EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest allowed for temperatures to reach or exceed 110 degrees during the early part of July. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 18 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three heat-associated fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances and location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system spread considerable moisture into the central Arizona deserts on March 12th, and the combination of strong forcing, deep moisture and modest instability resulted in scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. The storms resulted in areas of moderate to heavy rain, resulting in episodes of flooding and flash flooding that impacted areas near and around the greater Phoenix area. Heavy rain to the southwest of Phoenix led to the morning closure of Highway 238. Afternoon flash flooding near Wittmann led to the closure of Dove Valley Road due to debris. Evening flash flooding southwest of Buckeye and west of Highway 85 led to flowing streams and washes that exceeded action stages. Flowing water led to the closure of old Highway 80 in multiple locations. Multiple Flash Flood Warnings were issued during the day as a result of the heavy rain and flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system spread considerable moisture into the central deserts on March 12th, and aided by modest instability it generated scattered showers and thunderstorms. Some of the stronger storms produced heavy rain sufficient to lead to an episode of flash flooding near the town of Wittmann and to the northwest of Surprise. According to a public report from the town of Surprise, at 1000MST flash flooding occurred about 1 mile northeast of Wittmann. Flooding caused the closure of Dove Valley Road between 203rd and 211th Avenues. The closure was due to debris across the road. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect at the time of the flooding but there were no reports of accidents or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the central deserts during the afternoon hours on March 18th. The storms impacted portions of the greater Phoenix area. Locally heavy rain developed in a line from Sun City West southwestward through the town of Buckeye; peak rain rates were in excess of one inch per hour and resulted in the potential for flash flooding. One Flash Flood Warning was issued for the western portion of the Phoenix area. To the east of Mesa, thunderstorms generated wind gusts in excess of 60 mph that led to multiple trees being blown down. Additionally, at about 1500MST storms in the community of Maricopa dropped dime to penny sized hail. There were no reports of damage due to the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, some with heavy rain, developed across the western portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on March 18th. Peak rain rates were observed to be well in excess of one inch per hour at times. At 1700MST a trained spotter located 1 mile west of Citrus Park measured 0.80 inches within a 45 minute period. At 1647MST there was a public report that indicated 1.11 inches of rain fell within a short period near Indian School Road and 195th Avenue. Most of the rain fell within a ten minute period; 0.83 inches was measured between 1617MST and 1627MST. The rain was recorded at a flood control district automated gauge near Jackrabbit Trail and Indian School  Road. Although flash flooding was not reported, a Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1637MST and the warning continued through 2030MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weakening, but still strong upper level low pressure system moved east across the Phoenix area on March 13th, it was able to interact with a moist and unstable air mass and produce isolated to scattered thunderstorms. For the most part the storms were not especially strong, but a couple of them managed to produce both large hail and a funnel cloud. The funnel cloud, which occurred about 5 miles to the southeast of Wittmann, was observed by a trained spotter and while it did not produce any damage it was notable for its rarity as well as media interest. The large hail occurred about 6 miles to the northeast of the town of Surprise; hailstones approached one inch in diameter but did not produce any reported damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the early afternoon hours on March 13th. Some of the stronger storms produced a bit of large hail, which impacted communities such as  Peoria and Surprise. According to a trained spotter located about 6 miles northeast of Surprise, at 1235MST nickel sized hail fell in Peoria near the intersection of Lake Pleasant Parkway and Happy Valley Road. The diameter of the larger stones was about 0.88 inches; no damage was reported due to the large hail.

The storms resulted in areas of moderate to heavy rain, resulting in episodes of flooding and flash flooding that impacted areas near and around the greater Phoenix area. Heavy rain to the southwest of Phoenix led to the morning closure of Highway 238. Afternoon flash flooding near Wittmann led to the closure of Dove Valley Road due to debris. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system spread deep moisture into the central deserts on Marcth 12th; aided by modest instability it allowed scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours. Moderate to heavy rain fell with many of the storms, affecting areas to the southwest of central Phoenix. Peak rain rates were well in excess of 1 inch per hour; earlier in the day at about 1100MST a rain gage at Woolsey Peak measured 2.37 inches in a 2 hour period. The heavy rain led to some flash flooding as streams and washes rose rapidly, exceeding alarm stages on gages and causing road closures. At 2303MST county officials reported that Agua Caliente Road was closed as the Cruff Wash reached action stage. The wash crested at flood stage of 4.8 feet. This was about 9 miles south of the Palo Verge Nuclear Generating Station. Shortly after that, at 2324MST, county officials reported that Old Highway 80 was closed at Rainbow Wash as the wash reached action stage. This was about 15 miles southwest of Buckeye. Finally, at 2356MST officials reported that Old Highway 80 was closed due to the Centennial Wash reaching action stage; this occurred about 10 miles south-southeast of the Palo Verde station. Flash Flood Warnings were issued and in effect at the time of the road closures. Fortunately there were no reports of injuries or accidents due to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across the central deserts and to the northwest of Phoenix during the late morning hours on March 13th. One of the stronger storms generated a funnel cloud. According to a trained spotter located about 5 miles to the southeast of the town of Wittmann, at 1025MST a funnel cloud was observed along Highway 60 between Patton Road and Happy Valley Road. Although the funnel did not produce any damage, it was noteworthy due to its rarity and high media interest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of the east valley during the afternoon hours on March 18th and one of the stronger storms produced gusty and damaging winds that impacted East Mesa. According to a trained weather spotter, at about 1553MST a storm generated wind gusts in excess of 60 mph; the gusts blew down multiple trees about 3 miles northeast of East Mesa or just southeast of Falcon Field Municipal Airport. Some of the downed trees had trunks with diameters of around 8 inches. There were no reports of injury due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system spread considerable amounts of moisture into the deserts of Arizona on March 12th. The low, aided by an unstable air mass, generated widespread showers along with embedded thunderstorms. Moderate to heavy rain developed during the afternoon and evening across portions of La Paz County which resulted in an episode of flooding in far eastern La Paz County. Around 2200MST county officials reported that Centennial Wash exceeded alarm stage which would lead to flooding in the area. There were no reports of accidents due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific upper level low spread considerable moisture into the southwest Arizona deserts on March 12th, resulting in the development of scattered to numerous showers during afternoon and evening hours. There were embedded thunderstorms that helped generate heavy rain over portions of La Paz County. The heavy rain resulted in an episode of flooding over eastern portions of the county. According to county officials, at 2208MST Centennial Wash exceeded alarm stage due to flooding. This occurred near the town of Wenden. There were no reports of accidents due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of high pressure parked over the Southwest United States led to continued excessive heat conditions across the region from the end of July into the beginning of August. Temperatures each day climbed near or above 110 degrees with a daily record high of 114 degrees being set in Phoenix on the 3rd. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 40 heat-associated fatalities occurred with this event for the beginning of August.

Thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, mainly to the southeast of Phoenix, during the afternoon hours on September 8th. Some of the stronger storms produced gusty and damaging winds that approached 60 mph in strength. The strong winds downed multiple trees in the community of Gilbert, just to the southwest of Mesa-Gateway Airport. Small to moderate hailstones were also reported; at roughly 1700MST there was a report of hailstones about 0.88 inches in diameter near the intersection of Power and Germann Roads. There were no reports of injury due to the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with moderate hail developed in the community of Higley during the afternoon hours on September 8th. At roughly 1700MST hail with diameters up to 0.88 inches fell about 3 miles southeast of Higley and just to the southwest of the Mesa-Gateway airport. The hail, which fell near the intersection of Power and Germann Road, was reported via a CoCoRaHS report. There was no damage reported due to the hail.

Wind gusts were estimated to be as high as 60 mph; at about 1705MST a wind gust to 58 mph was reported from the nearby AWOS weather station at Phoenix Mesa-Gateway airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western United States allowed for high temperatures to top out nearly 10 degrees above normal at the end of the month. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, two heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One heat-associated fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances and location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening but still potent upper level low pressure system continued to push east across Arizona during the day on March 13th, and the atmosphere across the central deserts was still very humid and a bit unstable. As the system moved across the greater Phoenix area, scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed over the greater Phoenix area. Some of the thunderstorms generated very heavy rains; a trained spotter near the town of Surprise measured 0.7 inches of rain within a 10 minute period. The heavy rain resulted in episodes of street flooding as well as flash flooding. At about 1700MST flash flooding on Cave Creek near 7th Avenue and Greenway Road. Water 6 inches deep was flowing across 7th Avenue as reported by the public. Heavy rain 3 miles south of Deer Valley caused flash flooding at the Interstate 17 and Greenway underpass to flood. Water 1 to 2 feet deep filled up the underpass resulting in at least one stalled car. A Flash Flood Warning was issued and in effect at the time of the underpass flooding. There were no reports of injuries due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms, some with heavy rain, occurred during the afternoon hours across the deserts to the west of Phoenix on March 13th. The heavy rain caused washes, streams and rivers to flow very heavily, posing significant danger to motorists. According to local broadcast media, at 1930MST a motorist became trapped in his vehicle at Baseline Road and 319th Avenue due to the Hassayampa River flowing swiftly across the road. The Hassayampa was swollen and flowing heavily due to the earlier heavy rain in the area. About 20 minutes later, a water rescue was needed for the motorist; the rescue occurred to the west of the town of Buckeye. There were no reports of injuries to either the motorists or the rescuers. There was no flash flood warning in effect at the time of the water rescue.

A Flash Flood Warning was issued for the area at 1530MST and it was in effect at the time of the flooding. There were no reports of any injuries fortunately.

Thunderstorms developed across portions of southern Gila County during the afternoon hours on August 1st. One of the stronger storms produced large hail with diameters of about one inch. The large hailstones fell in the town of Peridot, and in one instance window damage was reported due to the hail. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued shortly after the large hail reports. No injuries were reported due to the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with large hail developed near the town of Peridot in southern Gila County during the afternoon hours on August 1st. At 1633MST, a teacher at a local school in Peridot reported quarter sized hail. Some of the largest stones had a diameter of one inch and were large enough to break a window at one of the residences on campus. At about the same time, penny to nickle sized hailstones were reported by the public in town, at the Bashas store along US Highway 70. No damage or injuries were reported from those hailstones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed to the southeast of Phoenix and over portions of northern Pinal County during the evening hours on August 11th. Some of the stronger storms generated gusty outflow winds that reached to at least 40 mph; the winds were sufficient to cause areas of blowing dust that lowered visibility to one quarter mile or below. From about 1900MST to 1930MST, multiple spotter and public reports were taken between Casa Grande and the Ak Chin Indian Reservation indicating dense blowing dust lowering visibility to one quarter mile or below. There were no dust storm warnings issued and no accidents or injuries resulted from the severely restricted visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed to the southeast of Phoenix during the evening hours on August 11th and some of the stronger storms generated gusty outflow winds which reached to about 40 mph. The winds were strong enough to generate areas of dense blowing dust that impacted portions of northwest Pinal County, from Casa Grande northwest to the Ak Chin Indian Reservation. At 1926MST, a member of the public located about 1 mile southeast of the town of Maricopa reported less than one quarter of a mile visibility in a dust storm. At about the same time, a trained weather spotter 4 miles north of Casa Grande reported visibility down to one eighth of a mile in a dust storm. A bit earlier at about 1910MST, the official AWOS weather station at the Ak Chin Airport measured one quarter of a mile visibility in blowing dust with wind gusts to 38 mph. A Dust Storm Warning was not issued; fortunately there were no reports of accidents or injury due to the dust storm and the sharply restricted visibilities.

According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 40 heat-associated fatalities occurred with this event for the beginning of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six heat-associated fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances and location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon thunderstorms developed across portions of the south central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on August 16th. Some of the stronger storms generated very strong and gusty outflow winds that approached 80 mph in strength at times. At about 2050MST a traveler on Interstate 8 reported significant roof, tree and car damage from a collapsing thunderstorm near the Sentinel rest area. Earlier, at about 1745MST a member of the public located 7 miles northeast of Wittmann reported about 20 trees blown down on a local golf course. The larger trees had diameters of about 12 inches, and the damage was consistent with wind gusts of at least 60 mph. There were no reports of injuries due to the strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong, damaging outflow winds developed near the town of Wittmann during late afternoon hours on August 16th. At about 1744MST, a member of the public located 7 miles northeast of Wittmann reported that strong winds blew down about 20 trees at a local golf course. The diameter of the largest trees was about 12 inches. Peak wind gusts were estimated by radar to be at least 60 mph in strength. The gusty winds also did additional damage such as to fencing, as reported by the course manager. There were no reports of injury due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening but still potent upper level low pressure system continued to push east across Arizona during the day on March 13th, and the atmosphere across the central deserts was still very humid and a bit unstable. As the system moved across the greater Phoenix area, scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed over the greater Phoenix area. Some of the thunderstorms generated very heavy rains; a trained spotter near the town of Surprise measured 0.7 inches of rain within a 10 minute period. The heavy rain resulted in episodes of street flooding as well as flash flooding. At about 1700MST flash flooding on Cave Creek near 7th Avenue and Greenway Road. Water 6 inches deep was flowing across 7th Avenue as reported by the public. Heavy rain 3 miles south of Deer Valley caused flash flooding at the Interstate 17 and Greenway underpass to flood. Water 1 to 2 feet deep filled up the underpass resulting in at least one stalled car. A Flash Flood Warning was issued and in effect at the time of the underpass flooding. There were no reports of injuries due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An exiting but still potent upper level disturbance acted upon a still wet and unstable air mass to produce scattered afternoon thunderstorms across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area on March 13th. Some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rain with peak rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. Portions of the heavy rain fell in the community of Buckeye resulting in an episode of flash flooding. According to a trained weather spotter located about 3 miles north of the town of Buckeye, at 1605MST flash flooding resulted in flowing water 6 inches deep covering a 20 foot wide swath across Miller Road, just south of lower Buckeye Road. Despite the quickly flowing water there were no reports of any accidents due to the flash flooding.

Thunderstorms developed over the deserts to the west of Phoenix during the afternoon hours on July 23rd and some of the stronger storms generated locally heavy rainfall. The heavy rain was sufficient to lead to an episode of flash flooding to the southwest of Phoenix, between Gila Bend and Buckeye, just to the west of Highway 85. The flash flooding resulted in the closure of old Highway 80 about 15 miles southwest of Buckeye. The highway was closed by Rainbow Wash and the flooding was captured on video by a trained spotter. There were no reports of accidents or injury due to the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed to the southwest of Phoenix during the afternoon hours on July 23rd, resulting in an episode of flash flooding approximately 15 miles to the southwest of the town of Buckeye. Flash flooding resulted in the closure of old US 80 at Rainbow Wash at about 1515MST. The wash quickly rose over 3.5 feet. The timing of the flooding was based on stream gage data from the Maricopa County Flood Control network. Flash flooding was observed on video by a trained weather spotter; he could see the flood waters moving through the wash and approaching highway 80. There were no reports of accidents or injury due to the flash flooding.

Very hot temperatures were experienced in southeast Arizona from July 29th through August 1st. Many locations approached or exceeded 110 degrees and set new daily record high temperatures. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two adult males on the Tohono O'odham Nation on July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 7 to 16 degrees above normal occurred in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category, which was attained July 29 through July 31, to continue on August 1.  High temperatures on August 1 included 111 degrees at Picacho and Fort Thomas; 108 at Ajo, Green Valley (tie) and Willcox (record); and 105 (tie) at Douglas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Light Fire started on August 28th on San Carlos Apache Tribe lands between Calva and Bylas in northwest Graham County.  The fire spread through salt cedar by strong winds and burned around 1000 acres before becoming contained August 31st. Fire suppression costs tallied $50,000.  Five homes were evacuated near Calva Road and several utility poles burned, but no homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The human caused Light Fire started on August 28th on San Carlos Apache Tribe lands between Calva and Bylas in northwest Graham County.  The fire spread through salt cedar by strong winds and burned around 1000 acres with nearly 500 foot high flames, before becoming contained August 31st. Fire suppression costs tallied $50,000.  Five homes were evacuated near Calva Road and several utility poles burned, but no homes were damaged. The fire burned under the Bylas bridge causing the bridge and Highway 70 to be closed for 16 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the afternoon.  One cluster of storms tracked north across western Pima County and downed power poles in Ajo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several power poles in Ajo, causing a power outage for about 100 customers for 2 to 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed and moved west across parts of southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced a severe wind gust at Nogales Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KOLS ASOS at Nogales Airport measured a wind gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved southwest over southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced wind damage in Amado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KALK ASOS at Pioneer Field recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved southwest over southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced wind damage in Amado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced damaging winds that downed several trees in Amado.  One of the trees downed a power line that caused a power outage to 200 customers for two hours. Winds also ripped a gate from its hinges at one residence. Additionally, a wind gust to 78 mph was recorded at the Hopkins RAWS station at 7100 feet MSL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved west across the Tohono O'odham Nation during the afternoon.  One storm produced damaging winds in South Komelik.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind blew the roof off a home in South Komelik.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed and moved west across parts of southeast Arizona. One storm produced damaging winds that downed power poles near Santa Rosa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed nine power poles south of Santa Rosa. Power was out in  several villages for two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved northeast across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced wind damage at Marana High School and another produced flash flooding over the Bighorn Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused damage at Marana High School. Several trees were uprooted and two light poles were downed which caused damage to fencing on the tennis courts. A large set of bleachers 100 feet long and 20 feet tall was destroyed when it was thrown 50 feet onto the football field causing significant damage to the playing surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved northeast across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced wind damage at Marana High School and another produced flash flooding over the Bighorn Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch of rain fell in less than an hour over the headwaters of the Bighorn Fire burn scar on the Santa Catalina Mountains. An 8 foot rise was noted along the Canada Del Oro Wash  at Rancho Solano upstream in Pinal County . Strong flows continued downstream into Pima County causing water to flow across Edwin Road.  Catalina St. Park Road was also closed due to flows in Sutherland Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3) category, which began in late August and expanded in September, expanded further in October to include all of the Tohono O'odham Nation and all of Santa Cruz and Cochise counties, except for the Huachuca Mountains. Extreme drought continued across all of southeast Arizona north and east of these locations and the exceptional drought (D4) category began in eastern portions of Pima and Pinal counties and western Graham County. Tucson recorded its fifth warmest October on record and while most of October was dry and warmer than normal, Tucson actually recorded its 4th earliest fall freeze on record, which was brought on by an early season winter storm that also produced low elevation snow during the last week of the month. Nevertheless, precipitation totals from this storm were generally less than a quarter inch in the valleys with around a half inch for the Mogollon Rim and some of the sky islands, and at less than 30 percent of normal precipitation for the month, were not enough to stop drought conditions from deteriorating further. The lightning-caused Cow Canyon Fire, that started in August, continued to spread in Greenlee County, consuming over 35,000 acres due to hot, dry and breezy conditions before being slowed by the early season snow. Meanwhile, several human caused wildfires took hold during October.  The largest was the Encinos Fire which consumed nearly 15,000 acres along and east of the Baboquivari Mountains in Pima County. Soil moisture readings remained  well below normal, falling to below the 5th percentile ranking in most areas, while remaining just below the 10th percentile ranking near the New Mexico state line. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell to less than 3 percent of storage capacity by the end of the month.

Extreme drought continued across all of southeast Arizona north and east of these locations and the exceptional drought (D4) category began in eastern portions of Pima and Pinal counties and western Graham County. Tucson recorded its fifth warmest October on record and while most of October was dry and warmer than normal, Tucson actually recorded its 4th earliest fall freeze on record, which was brought on by an early season winter storm that also produced low elevation snow during the last week of the month. Nevertheless, precipitation totals from this storm were generally less than a quarter inch in the valleys with around a half inch for the Mogollon Rim and some of the sky islands, and at less than 30 percent of normal precipitation for the month, were not enough to stop drought conditions from deteriorating further. The lightning-caused Cow Canyon Fire, that started in August, continued to spread in Greenlee County, consuming over 35,000 acres due to hot, dry and breezy conditions before being slowed by the early season snow. Meanwhile, several human caused wildfires took hold during October.  The largest was the Encinos Fire which consumed nearly 15,000 acres along and east of the Baboquivari Mountains in Pima County. Soil moisture readings remained  well below normal, falling to below the 5th percentile ranking in most areas, while remaining just below the 10th percentile ranking near the New Mexico state line. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell to less than 3 percent of storage capacity by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures were experienced on October 6th with high temperatures just over 100 degrees in central Pima County. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one male found in the desert southeast of Cowlic on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures 10 to 15 degrees above normal, just over 100 degrees, occurred on the Tohono O'odham Nation causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on October 6th. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one male near southeast of Cowlic.

The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one male found in the desert near the International Border west northwest of San Miguel on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures 10 to 15 degrees above normal, in the middle to upper 90s, occurred on the Tohono O'odham Nation causing the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on October 20th. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one male near the International Border west northwest of San Miguel.

Very hot temperatures were experienced in parts of southeast Arizona from June 3rd through June 5th with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. No heat related fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 5 to 12 degrees above normal occurred in the western deserts of Pima and southeast Pinal counties, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. A number of daily high temperature records were tied or broken. On June 3rd, Ajo set a new record high with 108 degrees while San Manuel topped out one degree shy of a record at 103 degrees. The Tucson International Airport was two shy with a high of 105. Meanwhile, Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument and Picacho both reached 107 and Sells hit 106. On June 4th, Ajo tied a record with 110 degrees as did San Manuel with 104. The Tucson International Airport reached 108 degrees and Sells climbed to 107.

Very hot temperatures were experienced in parts of southeast Arizona from June 3rd through June 5th with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. No heat related fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 6 to 13 degrees above normal occurred in the valleys of Graham, Cochise and southern Greenlee counties, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. A number of daily high temperature records were tied or broken. On June 3rd, highs included 106 degrees at both the Safford Airport and the Ag Station, 105 at Cascabel and 104 at Duncan. On June 4th, both observing locations in Safford set record highs with 108 degrees while Duncan topped out one degree shy of a record at 107. Cascabel reached 108 and San Simon hit 104. On June 5th, new record highs included 111 degrees at Safford Ag Station, 110 at Safford Airport, 108 at San Simon, 107 at Duncan and 106 at McNeal.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a foot of snow to the high country of northern Greenlee County from the afternoon of the 18th into the early morning hours of the 19th. This weather system also brought light to moderate snow to other mountains in southeast Arizona and general rain amounts of a half to one inch across the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on the afternoon of the 18th and became heavy at times, with a foot of snow accumulation reported by mid evening according to the Beaver Head SNOTEL. Snow continued into the early morning hours of the 19th. Total accumulations of 10 to 13 inches were recorded and ADOT reported a foot of snow on Highway 191.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure brought windy conditions to northern Arizona on March 26. The strongest winds were felt downwind of the White Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at the Springerville Airport measured sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher between 815 AM and Noon MST.  The peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured at 955 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in parts of southeast Arizona on May 6th and 7th with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. No heat related fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot spring temperatures 12 to 17 degrees above normal occurred in parts of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Several daily high temperature records were tied or broken.  On May 6th record highs included 107 degrees at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, 106 at Tucson International Airport, 104 at Ajo (tie) and Sells, 103 at Safford Ag Center, 102 at Sasabe and Benson, 100 at Nogales Airport and 98 at San Manuel.  On May 7th record highs included 108 degrees at Ajo, 104 at Green Valley, 102 at Safford Airport, 101 at Sasabe, 98 at Nogales Airport and 97 at San Manuel. Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument (107) and Picacho (105) were both shy of a record on May 7th by one degree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Willow Beach Access Road was closed 1600 MST due to severe damage from flash flooding. One car was stuck in the debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The measured peak wind gust in Bellemont was 65 MPH at 345 PM.  A large pondreosa pine tree snapped off in northwest Flagstaff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough just off the West Coast will combined with a high pressure circulation over southeast Arizona increase the pressure gradient and moisture over northern Arizona. A thunderstorm combined with the gradient wind to produce high wind gusts in Winslow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was recorded at the Winslow Airport at 555 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot conditions were present across the central Arizona deserts on May 30th; high temperatures in the Phoenix area reached to around 110 degrees. There was modest mid level moisture streaming into eastern Arizona and the combination of moisture and excessive heat led to isolated afternoon thunderstorms to the southeast of Phoenix. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph resulted in dust storm conditions across portions of the southeast valley as well as northwest Pinal County. At 1820MST visibility dropped to 1/8 of a mile near Interstate 10 and Casa Blanca Road, and shortly after trained spotters in Higley reported less than one quarter of a mile in a dust storm. There were no reports of accidents due to the sharply restricted visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot conditions along with limited mid level moisture combined to provide an environment favorable for high based thunderstorm development in the central deserts. Isolated storms formed southeast of Phoenix during late afternoon hours and one of the stronger storms generated gusty outflow winds which led to a dust storm across northwest Pinal County. At 1820MST, a trained weather spotter located about 3 miles southwest of Santan reported visibility down to 1/8 of a mile in a dust storm near Interstate 10 and Casa Blanca Road. About one half hour later a Dust Storm Warning was issued. There were no reports of accidents due to the sharply restricted visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot conditions were present across the central Arizona deserts on May 30th; high temperatures in the Phoenix area reached to around 110 degrees. There was modest mid level moisture streaming into eastern Arizona and the combination of moisture and excessive heat led to isolated afternoon thunderstorms to the southeast of Phoenix. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph resulted in dust storm conditions across portions of the southeast valley as well as northwest Pinal County. At 1820MST visibility dropped to 1/8 of a mile near Interstate 10 and Casa Blanca Road, and shortly after trained spotters in Higley reported less than one quarter of a mile in a dust storm. There were no reports of accidents due to the sharply restricted visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures and modest mid level moisture combined to produce an atmosphere conducive to thunderstorm development and during late afternoon, isolated high based storms developed southeast of Phoenix. One of the storms generated gusty outflow winds that resulted in a dust storm across the east valley. According to a trained spotter located about 3 miles northeast of Higley, at 1900MST visibility fell to less than one quarter of a mile along Recker Road between Warner and Elliot roads. A Dust Storm Warning had been issued for the area 5 minutes earlier. There were no reports of accidents due to the sharply restricted visibility.

According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, five heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.

Scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the morning hours on May 10th. Due to modest instability, the storms typically generated wind gusts below 50 mph that were non-damaging. There were a few reports from automated mesonet gages that reported wind gusts around 45 mph including one such gage 3 miles northeast of Glendale and another near the town of Waddell. At 0730MST the public reported that wind gusts around 45 mph snapped multiple trees in the area around Waddell and the damage was displayed via social media reports and pictures. There were no reports of injury due to the falling trees or strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix area during the morning hours on March 10th and they affected communities such as Waddell and Surprise. Some of the stronger storms generated wind gusts upwards of 45 mph; according to a report from the public, at 0730MST the gusty winds snapped several trees in the area about 1 mile northeast of Waddell. Pictures of the snapped trees were displayed in social media posts. A nearby weather station measured a wind gust to 45 mph at the time of the tree damage. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in parts of southeast Arizona May 28th through the 31st with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. No heat-related fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 7 to 13 degrees above normal occurred in parts of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Several daily high temperature records were tied or broken. Record highs included: May 28th, 107 degrees (tie) at Ajo and 106 at Duncan and Safford Airport; May 29th, 108 degrees at Tucson International Airport, 107 at Fort Thomas and 102 (tie) at San Manuel; May 30th, 111 degrees at Ajo, 109 at Picacho and 106 (tie) at Anvil Ranch; May 31st, 106 degrees (tie) at Green Valley.   On May 29th, Sells reached 108 degrees, which was two degrees shy of a record while Picacho topped out at 110, just one degree off the record for the date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced gusty winds across Santa Cruz and Eastern Pima counties during the afternoon of May 30th.  One storm produced a severe wind gust at Tucson International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dissipated near Interstate 19 creating thunderstorm outflow winds that produced a gust of 69 mph at Tucson International Airport. Airport tower personnel reported that the building shook as a result of the winds and blowing dust briefly reduced visibility near the south end of the airfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the Mojave Desert helped lift an unstable air mass, producing scattered thunderstorms. Isolated flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AZ Highway 95 was closed near 3rd Street due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in parts of southeast Arizona on May 6th and 7th with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. No heat related fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot spring temperatures 12 to 17 degrees above normal occurred in parts of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. Several daily high temperature records were tied or broken.  On May 6th record highs included 107 degrees at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, 106 at Tucson International Airport, 104 at Ajo (tie) and Sells, 103 at Safford Ag Center, 102 at Sasabe and Benson, 100 at Nogales Airport and 98 at San Manuel.  On May 7th record highs included 108 degrees at Ajo, 104 at Green Valley, 102 at Safford Airport, 101 at Sasabe, 98 at Nogales Airport and 97 at San Manuel. Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument (107) and Picacho (105) were both shy of a record on May 7th by one degree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures occurred on May 26th across the Tohono O'odham Nation. One heat-related fatality occurred east of Sells.

High temperatures included 100 degrees at Sells, and 98 at Sasabe, Anvil Ranch and the Tucson International Airport. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one adult male 2 miles east of Sells, about 2 miles south of Tohono O'odham Community College.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to portions of northern Arizona on February 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust at the Show Low Airport Show Low was 64 MPH at 1035 AM. The winds gusted over 58 MPH between 1015 AM and 3 PM. The sustained winds were above 40 MPH between 930 AM and 430 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain, moderate to heavy at times, occurred on Saturday February 22nd and the excessive rain eventually led to episodes of areal flooding across higher terrain areas to the east of Phoenix. One of the more problematic areas was the Tonto Creek basin; rainfall amounts of 3 inches or more along the basin and upstream caused areal flooding to occur and forced the closure of several low water crossings. Rainfall diminished during the afternoon and evening on the 22nd allowing streamflow to level out somewhat but a new round of heavy showers occurred during late evening Saturday and into the early morning on the 23rd. The new round of moderate to heavy showers occurring on an already saturated ground led to a spike in stream flow and produced flash flooding near Punkin center shortly after midnight. According to broadcast media, at about 0140MST a motorist was rescued while trying to cross Tonto Creek at the store crossing in Punkin Center. An Areal Flood Warning remained in effect for the area at the time of the rescue. No injuries were reported, fortunately.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain across the high terrain east of Phoenix on Saturday February 22nd led to  areal flooding in the Tonto Creek basin north of Lake Roosevelt. Early in the morning on February 23rd, a fresh batch of showers and weak thunderstorms occurred which produced another period of moderate to heavy rain. The additional rain fell in a short period on already saturated ground and led to a rapid rise in the streamflow on Tonto Creek and as a result flash flooding occurred. According to local broadcast media, at 0140MST a motorist needed to be rescued while trying to cross Tonto Creek at the store crossing in Punkin center. The motorist was not injured. While a flash flood warning was not in effect at the time of the rescue, an areal flood warning remained in effect for the area; the warning was issued during the middle of the day on Saturday the 22nd.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to portions of northern Arizona on February 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds were felt across the Little Colorado River Valley Apache County. Sustained winds over 40 MPH were felt for over 9 hours in St Johns and Springerville. Peak wind gusts include Springervile 78 MPH at 135 PM, St Johns 70 MPH at 243 PM, and Lupton 60 MPH at 212 PM. There were multiple reports of downed power poles in Springerville and Eagar.  Trees were blown down in Concho and Show Low.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low moved over northern Arizona with the heaviest snow over Yavapai County and southern Apache counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell in Walker at an elevation of 6,400 feet.  Nine inches of snow fell in Crown King at an elevation of 5,800 feet.  Four to eight inches of snow fell in the Prescott and Prescott Valley area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low moved over northern Arizona with the heaviest snow over Yavapai County and southern Apache counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to nine inches of snow fell in the Alpine area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level closed low centered off the coast of Southern California brought abundant moisture into Arizona from the southwest. Areas of heavy rain fell south of Interstate 40 and east of Flagstaff. Light to moderate rain fell elsewhere with snow levels generally above 8000 feet.  A cold front then dropped snow levels to below 6000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were canyoneering in Bull Canyon (also known as Water Slide Canyon) near Cherry Creek when one was swept away by rushing waters. An inch and a half to two inches of rain had fallen in the drainage in the previous 11 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front brought high winds to the peaks just north of Flagstaff above 10,000 feet. The peak wind gust was 84 MPH at 11,555 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor on Agassiz Peak at 11,555 feet measured sustained winds above 40 MPH from 7 AM February 25 until 200 AM on February 26. Wind gusts above 58 MPH were measured from 320 AM until 200 AM.  The peak wind gusts of 84 MPH were measured between 11:10 and 11:20 AM (inclusive). The wind sensor at 9,969 feet measured a peak wind gust of 62 MPH at 11:00 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, wet Pacific upper level low pressure system spread copious amounts of moisture into south central Arizona on Saturday February 22nd; as a result widespread rain developed early in the morning and the moderate to heavy rain persisted for much of the day. The heaviest rainfall amounts occurred over higher terrain areas to the east of Phoenix; most of the rain gages in the area measured between 1.25 and 3 inches with the most significant rain occurring during the morning hours. The excessive rain resulted in a few episodes of areal flooding which included the Sycamore Creek and Tonto Creek basins. During the afternoon hours, local law enforcement reported multiple roads closed due to flooding in the Tonto Creek basin; the closures occurred to the northwest of Lake Roosevelt. An areal flood warning was issued for the basin due to elevated flow in the creek and the warning persisted for several days. In addition, during the evening hours a family of 5 drove into Sycamore Creek, which was flowing heavily, and became stranded necessitating a water rescue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system spread copious amounts of moisture into south-central Arizona on Saturday February 22nd; this lead to widespread moderate to heavy rain across the high terrain to the northeast of Phoenix. Significant amounts of rain fell in the Tonto Creek basin with gage reports in the area measuring as high as 3 inches during the event. This led to areal flooding in the basin which resulted in several low water crossings becoming inundated. At 1303MST local law enforcement reported a cross road at Tonto Creek closed due to flooding. Later in the afternoon, at about 1713MST, law enforcement reported that the Bar X crossing at Tonto Creek was closed due to the flooding. Both closures were reported by GCSO. In addition, the AHAPS stream gage called Tonto Creek at Roosevelt measured flow significantly above action stage on Saturday, and the elevated flow continued into the middle of the following week. An Areal Flood Warning was issued due to the elevated streamflow. Flow above action stage results in flooding of several low water crossings in the creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific upper low spread copious amounts of moisture into south-central Arizona on Saturday February 22nd; this led to widespread moderate to heavy rain across higher terrain areas to the northeast of Phoenix. The excessive rain resulted in some areal flooding during the afternoon and evening hours, affecting the Sycamore Creek basin. According to local broadcast media, at roughly 2115MST a family of 5 drove their vehicle into Sycamore Creek, about 7 miles to the north-northwest of Saguaro Lake. Due to flowing water in the creek, their vehicle became stuck and necessitated a water rescue. There were several other vehicles trapped on river banks   awaiting the waters to recede so that they could proceed. There were no reports of accidents, injuries or fatalities. At the time there were no flood warnings in effect for the area as heavy rain had ended a long time ago.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system spread copious amounts of moisture into south central Arizona during the day on Saturday February 22nd; this led to widespread moderate to heavy rain which began in the morning hours. Although rain rates were never overly high, the steady rain allowed for some locations to pick up upwards of 3 inches during the day and some areal flooding occurred as a result. Flooding occurred in the area around Globe; as reported by the Gila County Sheriff's Office at 1536MST flooding caused the Beer Tree Crossing at Pinal Creek to become closed. The closure was about 1 mile to the southeast of Globe. While there were no flood warnings issued, an areal flood advisory was put into effect shortly after the report of the closure at the Beer Tree Crossing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds produced blowing dust that restricted visibility to less than a quarter mile along Interstate 10 in northwest portions of the Tucson Metro area, temporarily halting traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed and moved north across southeast Arizona, primarily south of Interstate 10. One storm caused flash flooding in southern Cochise County and another produced severe wind gusts in the Empire Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in 26th St and Camino Seco in Tucson.  Lightning only caused a small hole in the roof, but fire caused considerable damage to at least one room in the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed and moved west northwest across southeast Arizona.  Lightning started a house fire in the Dove Mountain area north of Tucson while other storms kicked up blowing dust in southeast Pinal County reducing visibility on Interstate 10 to a half mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started an attic fire in a house near Dove Mountain and Golden Barrel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated slow and erratic moving thunderstorms occurred across parts of southeast Arizona from Tucson and Nogales eastward. One storm produced heavy rain that led to debris flows on the Bighorn Fire burn scar area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow of mud, ash, branches and brush was caused by rainfall between 0.75 and 1.25 in just over 30 minutes on the northern part of the Bighorn Fire burn scar. A flow of around 2 feet was measured at the upper reaches of the Canada Del Oro Wash and a debris flow as deep as 2 feet was also observed downstream crossing Edwin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Polles Fire (pronounced Poll-E) burned burning in the Mazatzal Wilderness on top of the Polles Mesa, approximately 11 miles west of Payson, AZ and 6 miles south of Pine-Strawberry, AZ.  It eventually burned 638 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire started on 7/3 as the result of lightning from isolated thunderstorms in the area. The fire is only accessible by helicopter and 2 hotshot crews were shuttled into the fire by helicopter on 7/3. High sustained winds out of the southwest and low humidity in the fire area caused increased fire behavior on the afternoon of 7/4 pushing the fire north and northeast. Airtankers and helicopters supported the hotshot crews on the fireline through the day. A pilot died on July 7 when his helicopter crashed while transporting supplies for fire fighting operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Thumb Butte Fire was started by lightning at around 6 AM on July 16 on the South Rim of the Grand Canyon. It eventually burned 8,354 acres before rain stopped the fire from moving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Thumb Butte Fire was caused by lightning burned 8,354 acres of Havasupai Nation and Grand Canyon National Park Land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved east across Cochise, Graham and Greenlee counties and some  produced severe wind gusts.  Outbuildings sustained thunderstorm wind damage west of Douglas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph occurred at Safford Airport.  The storm continued into Greenlee County and produced a gust of 59 mph at Guthrie RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in parts of southeast Arizona from June 3rd through June 5th with high temperatures exceeding established records in many locations. No heat related fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 5 to 12 degrees above normal occurred in the western deserts of Pima and southeast Pinal counties, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached. A number of daily high temperature records were tied or broken. On June 3rd, Ajo set a new record high with 108 degrees while San Manuel topped out one degree shy of a record at 103 degrees. The Tucson International Airport was two shy with a high of 105. Meanwhile, Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument and Picacho both reached 107 and Sells hit 106. On June 4th, Ajo tied a record with 110 degrees as did San Manuel with 104. The Tucson International Airport reached 108 degrees and Sells climbed to 107.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures occurred in southeast Arizona on June 21st with high temperatures around 105 degrees on the Tohono O'odham Nation. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one adult male south of Sells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures occurred in south central Pima County on June 21st. High temperatures included 106 degrees at Sells and 104 degrees at Sasabe. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one adult male 2 miles southwest of Topawa, on the Tohono O'odham Nation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture and unstable air moved over northern Arizona.  This triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Indian Route 15 between Sunrise Springs and Burnside. Water and mud covered the road between 5 and 10 inches deep. Time and location are best estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture and unstable air moved over northern Arizona.  This triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on Carrizo Creek reported a 2.4 foot rise in water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled a thunderstorm that caused wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Arizona Department of Transportation wind sensor near Petrified Forest National Park  reported a peak wind gust of 64 MPH along Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple people reported seeing a funnel cloud near the community of Indian Wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to parts of Arizona on July 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of between 1.00 and 2.20 inches of rain that fell in just over an hour in the Vernon area.  Hail (0.70 inch diameter) was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to parts of Arizona on July 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 0.82 inches of rainfall in 25 minutes in Forest Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Jackson Fire in the Santa Teresa Mountains in western Graham County on July 11th. The fire was spread by strong winds and consumed 2946 acres before becoming fully contained July 22nd. Fire suppression costs were estimated at $1 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Jackson Fire in the Santa Teresa Mountains southwest of Fort Thomas on July 11th. The fire was spread by strong winds and consumed 2946 acres before becoming fully contained July 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures occurred in southeast Arizona on July 5th with high temperatures just above 100 degrees in southeast Cochise County. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one adult male west of Douglas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures occurred in southeast Cochise County on July 5th. High temperatures included 101 degrees at Tombstone and 103 at McNeal. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for one adult male 5 miles west of Douglas.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Springerville Airport reported wind gust over 58 MPH between 1015 PM and 1115 PM MST. The peak wind gust of 61 MPH was recorded at 1030 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor on Greens Peak (10,118 feet MSO) reported wind gust 58 MPH or higher between 702 and 722 PM MST.  The peak wind of 59 MPH was reported at 722 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind at the Flagstaff Airport gusted over 58 MPH between around 1 PM and 4 PM MST. Wind gusts of 60 MPH were reported at 158 and 352 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in southeast Arizona from July 10th through July 13th.  Many locations exceeded 110 degrees and set new daily record high temperatures. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three adult males, one on the western reaches of the Tohono O'odham Nation, one west of Douglas and one near Kino Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 6 to 18 degrees above normal occurred in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached July 10 through July 13.  A number of daily high temperature records were tied or broken. Safford Airport recorded its highest temperature ever of 115 degrees on July 11 and set new daily record highs of 111 on July 10 and 112 on July 12.  Tucson International Airport set a new daily high temperature record of 113 on July 12, which was just one degree shy of the all time hottest temperature for July.  The Tucson Airport also recorded its highest minimum temperature ever when it only dropped to 89 degrees on July 13.  On July 11 new record highs included 113 degrees at Duncan, 110 at Fort Thomas, 109 at Willcox and 103 at Sierra Vista. On July 12 new record highs included 115 degrees (tie) at Ajo, 113 at Picacho, 112 at Fort Thomas, 110 at Nogales Airport, San Simon and Willcox, 109 at McNeal and 108 at San Manuel. On July 13 new record highs included 114 degrees at Fort Thomas, 113 (tie) at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, 109 at Green Valley (tie) and Willcox and 107 at San Simon (tie) and Tombstone. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three adult males, one near Kino Springs on the 10th, another 10 miles west of Douglas on the 11th, and a third near Sweetwater in the western part of the Tohono O'odham Nation on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures for January were observed on the Tohono O'odham Nation. One cold-related death occurred in the Baboquivari Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the 30s were observed with wind chills in the 20s during the predawn hours of January 7th. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that hypothermia was the primary cause of death for one adult male. The death occurred in the Baboquivari Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, rope-like tornado touched down in open desert east of Kingman, tracked north for eight miles, and dissipated without causing any damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous photos and videos, from the Mohave County Emergency Manager and social media among other sources, documented a weak tornado which touched down over open desert, moved one mile north over the course of eight minutes, and dissipated. The tornado did not cause any damage; thus, it was rated EF-0.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Bringham Fire in the White Mountains 22 miles north of Morenci, on June 6th. The fire spread rapidly, driven by multiple days of strong winds before becoming fully contained on July 29th after consuming a total of 23,142 acres. The cost of fighting the fire was estimated at over $9 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Bringham Fire in the White Mountains 22 miles north of Morenci, on June 6th. The fire spread rapidly, driven by multiple days of strong winds.  Over 30 miles of U.S. Highway 191 were closed to traffic for most of the duration of the wildfire.  By the end of the month, the fire consumed its maximum acreage of 23,142 acres, but was only 40% contained. Full containment of 100% did not occur until July 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in southeast Arizona from July 29th through August 1st. Many locations approached or exceeded 110 degrees and set new daily record high temperatures. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two adult males on the Tohono O'odham Nation on July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 7 to 18 degrees above normal occurred in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached July 29 through July 31. High temperatures on July 29 included 113 degrees at Ajo, 110 at Sells, and 109 at Picacho and Safford Airport. On July 30 highs included 115 degrees at Ajo, 113 (records) at Duncan and Safford Airport, 112 at Picacho, 111 (tie) at Sells, 110 at Tucson International Airport and 107 (record) at Nogales Airport.  On July 31 highs included 113 degrees at Ajo, 112 at Picacho and Fort Thomas (record), and 110 at Tucson International Airport and Duncan. Very hot temperatures continued on August 1st. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two adult males on the Tohono O'odham Nation on July 30th, one near Topawa and another near San Miguel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed and moved north across southeast Arizona, primarily south of Interstate 10. One storm caused flash flooding in southern Cochise County and another produced severe wind gusts in the Empire Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rolled northwest across the Tucson Metro area with thunderstorm outflow winds downing numerous trees and ten power poles.  Power was out for several hours for at least 12,000 customers due to downed poles and lines as well as damaged transformers.  Power poles were reported down at Broadway and Craycroft, Prince and Flowing Wells and Ina and Oracle.  Fallen trees caused damage to the Mission Palms Apartments near Orange Grove and La Cholla. Trees also fell onto a vehicle at River and Craycroft and two cars in Oro Valley. A wind gust to 59 mph was recorded at KDMA Davis-Monthan AFB ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Bighorn Fire on the west side of the Santa Catalina Mountains during the evening of June 5th. The fire spread rapidly over the subsequent days and weeks, driven by multiple days of strong winds before becoming fully contained on July 23rd. The fire consumed a total of 119,987 acres. Residents were evacuated at times and a total of nine firefighters sustained minor injuries while fighting the fire. While no property damage occurred, the cost of fighting the fire was estimated at $44 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Bighorn Fire on the west side of the Santa Catalina Mountains during the evening of June 5th. The fire spread rapidly over the subsequent days and weeks, driven by multiple days of strong winds. Residents of Summerhaven, nearby mountain communities and those on the periphery of the mountains from the Catalina Foothills to southern Pinal County and near Redington Road were evacuated at times, but no structures were damaged by the fire. Two firefighters sustained minor injuries battling the fire during July, bringing the total number of injured personnel fighting the fire to nine.  The fire became fully contained on July 23rd after a total of 119,978 acres burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved west across southeast Arizona from the predawn hours through early afternoon.  Some storms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in and around the Tucson Metro area, including the Bighorn Fire burn scar area. Several swift water rescues were performed. One man drowned after a rapid rise in the normally dry Santa Cruz River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around an inch of rain in 30 minutes fell during the early morning hours near the headwaters of Pima Wash and Ventana Canyon Wash, within the Bighorn Fire burn scar area. Additional heavy rain of 0.75 to 1.5 fell in the urbanized area downstream of the burn area later that morning which caused combined runoff sufficient to cause a 2 foot sooty flow in Ventana Canyon Wash at Sunrise Drive and the closure of Snyder Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3) category, which began in late August and expanded in September, expanded further in October to include all of the Tohono O'odham Nation and all of Santa Cruz and Cochise counties, except for the Huachuca Mountains. Extreme drought continued across all of southeast Arizona north and east of these locations and the exceptional drought (D4) category began in eastern portions of Pima and Pinal counties and western Graham County. Tucson recorded its fifth warmest October on record and while most of October was dry and warmer than normal, Tucson actually recorded its 4th earliest fall freeze on record, which was brought on by an early season winter storm that also produced low elevation snow during the last week of the month. Nevertheless, precipitation totals from this storm were generally less than a quarter inch in the valleys with around a half inch for the Mogollon Rim and some of the sky islands, and at less than 30 percent of normal precipitation for the month, were not enough to stop drought conditions from deteriorating further. The lightning-caused Cow Canyon Fire, that started in August, continued to spread in Greenlee County, consuming over 35,000 acres due to hot, dry and breezy conditions before being slowed by the early season snow. Meanwhile, several human caused wildfires took hold during October.  The largest was the Encinos Fire which consumed nearly 15,000 acres along and east of the Baboquivari Mountains in Pima County. Soil moisture readings remained  well below normal, falling to below the 5th percentile ranking in most areas, while remaining just below the 10th percentile ranking near the New Mexico state line. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell to less than 3 percent of storage capacity by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in southeast Arizona from July 10th through July 13th.  Many locations exceeded 110 degrees and set new daily record high temperatures. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three adult males, one on the western reaches of the Tohono O'odham Nation, one west of Douglas and one near Kino Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 6 to 18 degrees above normal occurred in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached July 10 through July 13.  A number of daily high temperature records were tied or broken. Safford Airport recorded its highest temperature ever of 115 degrees on July 11 and set new daily record highs of 111 on July 10 and 112 on July 12.  Tucson International Airport set a new daily high temperature record of 113 on July 12, which was just one degree shy of the all time hottest temperature for July.  The Tucson Airport also recorded its highest minimum temperature ever when it only dropped to 89 degrees on July 13.  On July 11 new record highs included 113 degrees at Duncan, 110 at Fort Thomas, 109 at Willcox and 103 at Sierra Vista. On July 12 new record highs included 115 degrees (tie) at Ajo, 113 at Picacho, 112 at Fort Thomas, 110 at Nogales Airport, San Simon and Willcox, 109 at McNeal and 108 at San Manuel. On July 13 new record highs included 114 degrees at Fort Thomas, 113 (tie) at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument, 109 at Green Valley (tie) and Willcox and 107 at San Simon (tie) and Tombstone. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for three adult males, one near Kino Springs on the 10th, another 10 miles west of Douglas on the 11th, and a third near Sweetwater in the western part of the Tohono O'odham Nation on the 13th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across a large part of central and southwest Tucson.  Around 2 inches of rain fell in 60-90 minutes from the Catalina Foothills near Alvernon Way west through Flowing Wells and south to Tucson Estates, Drexel Heights and the Pascua Pueblo. Rain totals of at least 3 to 4 inches in southwest Tucson caused numerous road closures, including San Xavier Rd near the Mission.  Interstate 10 at Sunset Road was also closed briefly. Multiple vehicles became stranded in flood waters and several swift water rescues occurred in midtown. Vehicle rescues were noted on Fort Lowell Rd. at Tuscon Blvd and Stone Ave. and also near Glenn and Fairview.  A man was also rescued from the Rillito River near the Tucson Mall.  Farther south and west, a truck became stuck in Camino De Oeste Wash at Lloyd Bush Dr.  A two to three foot wall of water progressed down the normally dry Santa Cruz River.  The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that the cause of death was drowning for a man pulled from the Santa Cruz River near Speedway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong and expansive area of high pressure centered near the Four Corners allowed temperatures to climb up to 115 degrees, leading to excessive heat conditions. Record highs were broken in Phoenix on the 4th (114 degrees), 5th (115 degrees), and 6th (113 degrees). According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 15 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this event.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3) category, which began in late August and expanded in September, expanded further in October to include all of the Tohono O'odham Nation and all of Santa Cruz and Cochise counties, except for the Huachuca Mountains. Extreme drought continued across all of southeast Arizona north and east of these locations and the exceptional drought (D4) category began in eastern portions of Pima and Pinal counties and western Graham County. Tucson recorded its fifth warmest October on record and while most of October was dry and warmer than normal, Tucson actually recorded its 4th earliest fall freeze on record, which was brought on by an early season winter storm that also produced low elevation snow during the last week of the month. Nevertheless, precipitation totals from this storm were generally less than a quarter inch in the valleys with around a half inch for the Mogollon Rim and some of the sky islands, and at less than 30 percent of normal precipitation for the month, were not enough to stop drought conditions from deteriorating further. The lightning-caused Cow Canyon Fire, that started in August, continued to spread in Greenlee County, consuming over 35,000 acres due to hot, dry and breezy conditions before being slowed by the early season snow. Meanwhile, several human caused wildfires took hold during October.  The largest was the Encinos Fire which consumed nearly 15,000 acres along and east of the Baboquivari Mountains in Pima County. Soil moisture readings remained  well below normal, falling to below the 5th percentile ranking in most areas, while remaining just below the 10th percentile ranking near the New Mexico state line. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell to less than 3 percent of storage capacity by the end of the month.

Meanwhile, several human caused wildfires took hold during October.  The largest was the Encinos Fire which consumed nearly 15,000 acres along and east of the Baboquivari Mountains in Pima County. Soil moisture readings remained  well below normal, falling to below the 5th percentile ranking in most areas, while remaining just below the 10th percentile ranking near the New Mexico state line. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell to less than 3 percent of storage capacity by the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cow Canyon Fire was ignited by lightning on August 18th southeast of Blue in Greenlee County. The fire grew only minimally for several weeks until hot, dry and breezy conditions caused more rapid growth toward the end of September, which continued into October when northerly winds sent smoke plumes aloft over the communities of Clifton and Morenci at times. While the fire continued well into November, the spread of the fire was halted by October 22nd after consuming 35,371 acres. Fire suppression costs were estimated at $2,850,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Encinos Fire started 11 miles northwest of Arivaca along the eastern slopes of the Baboquivari Mountains in southeast Pima County on October 6th. The fire spread to over 10,000 acres by October 10th driven by strong winds, hot temperatures and very dry fuel condition. The fire consumed a total of 14,905 acres before becoming fully contained October 26th. Fire suppression costs tallied $438,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures were experienced in the lower deserts of southeast Arizona from August 24th-27th. Many locations approached or exceeded 110 degrees and set new daily record high temperatures. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for four males in Pima County during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 9 to 12 degrees above normal occurred in the lower elevations of southeast Arizona, causing the High Heat Risk category to be reached August 24 through August 27. Record high temperatures were tied or set at most locations on multiple days during the period. The hottest record temperatures during this stretch were 115 degrees at Ajo on August 25; 113 at Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument on August 25; 111 at Picacho on the 24th; 110 at Sells on the 24th; and 107 at Tucson International Airport on the 25th. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for four males on August 25th, one on Ironwood National Forest grounds west of Avra Valley, one just west of there near Queen's Well on the Tohono O'odham Nation, and two near Ajo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch of rain fell in less than an hour over the headwaters of the Canada Del Oro Wash within the Bighorn Fire burn scar on the Santa Catalina Mountains. An 8 foot rise was noted downstream at Rancho Solano and strong flows continued downstream into Pima County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3) category was reached in late August across most of Graham County, northern and central Greenlee County, northwest Cochise County and eastern portions of Pima and Pinal counties, including San Carlos Apache tribal lands, the White Mountains, the Pinaleno Mountains and the Santa Catalina Mountains. Below normal precipitation occurred each month from April through August of 2020 and the summer was the hottest on record in many locations. Several lightning caused wildfires and at least one human caused wildfire spread rapidly due to dry fuels, and continued into September. This included the Cow Canyon Fire in Greenlee County, which started on August 18th, but smoldered in heavy fuels with very little growth during August. Soil moisture readings were well below normal and ranked below the 20th percentile. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell from around 15 percent in July to less than 7 percent of storage capacity by the end of August.
